items.question: Who uses confidence intervals in ML and Gen AI work?
items.answer: Data scientists, ML engineers, and experiment platform owners use CIs when reporting model metrics, A/B test outcomes, latency benchmarks, and human-evaluation scores so stakeholders see uncertainty alongside point estimates.

items.question: What is a confidence interval?
items.answer: A range [L, U] computed from sample data by a fixed rule such that, if that rule were applied to many independent samples, approximately (1 − α)×100% of the resulting intervals would contain the true population parameter.

items.question: When should you report a confidence interval instead of only a point estimate?
items.answer: Whenever a sample statistic drives a decision—model selection, shipping a variant, claiming an improvement in a paper—and the audience needs to know how much the estimate could shift with sampling variation.

items.question: Where does the width of a confidence interval come from?
items.answer: From the standard error of the estimate (driven by sample size and variability) multiplied by a critical value from the z or t distribution set by the chosen confidence level (1 − α).

items.question: Why is a 95% confidence level standard but not magical?
items.answer: It balances width and coverage for many applications (α = 0.05 aligns with common two-sided tests), but higher confidence (99%) widens the interval and lower confidence (90%) narrows it—choose based on how much uncertainty you can tolerate.

items.question: How do you construct a 95% CI for a population mean when σ is unknown?
items.answer: Compute x̄ and s from the sample, SE = s/√n, find t_{0.025, n−1}, and set the interval to x̄ ± t_{0.025, n−1} × SE.

items.question: Confidence interval vs credible interval: what question does each answer?
items.answer: A confidence interval answers a frequentist coverage question—how often the procedure captures the fixed θ across repeated samples. A credible interval answers a Bayesian question—what posterior probability mass lies between two bounds given data and a prior. Same-looking numbers, different interpretations.

items.question: z-interval vs t-interval for a mean: when does each apply?
items.answer: Use a z-interval when σ is known or n is large enough that t ≈ z. Use a t-interval when σ is unknown and estimated by s, especially for small or moderate n, because t critical values are larger and produce wider, better-calibrated intervals.

items.question: What if you double the sample size while variability stays the same?
items.answer: The standard error shrinks by a factor of √2, so a symmetric CI's half-width shrinks by about 1/√2 ≈ 0.71—same confidence level, tighter range, because more data reduces sampling uncertainty.

items.question: What if you switch from a 95% to a 99% confidence level with the same data?
items.answer: The critical value increases (e.g. t_{0.005, df} > t_{0.025, df}), so the interval widens even though the point estimate is unchanged—you pay for higher nominal coverage with a less precise range.
